questions.question: What is the mechanism of action of aspirin?
questions.ground_truth: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes (COX-1 and COX-2), preventing the synthesis of prostaglandins and thromboxane A2. This results in anti-inflammatory, analgesic, antipyretic, and antiplatelet effects.
contexts: Aspirin acts by irreversibly acetylating the cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes.

questions.question: Describe the pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.
questions.ground_truth: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus is characterized by insulin resistance in peripheral tissues (muscle, liver, adipose) and progressive beta-cell dysfunction leading to relative insulin deficiency. Hyperglycemia results from increased hepatic glucose production, decreased glucose uptake in muscles, and impaired insulin secretion.
contexts: Type 2 diabetes involves a combination of insulin resistance and beta-cell failure.

questions.question: What are the main differences between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria?
questions.ground_truth: Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan cell wall that retains the crystal violet stain (appearing purple). Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer surrounded by an outer membrane containing lipopolysaccharide (LPS), staining pink with safranin. Gram-negative bacteria are generally more resistant to antibiotics due to their outer membrane.
contexts: The Gram stain differentiates bacteria based on cell wall composition.
